01a_quotes.title: Generative AI at Work

study_design: We study the staggered introduction of a generative AI-based conversational assistant using data from 5,179 customer support agents.
We examine the staggered deployment of a chat assistant using data from 5,000 agents working for a Fortune 500 software firm that provides business process software.
We isolate the causal impact of access to AI recommendations using a standard difference-in-differences regression
The AI assistant we study was gradually rolled out at the agent level after an initial seven-week randomized pilot featuring 50 agents.

A rapidly growing literature has shown that two-way fixed effects regressions deliver consistent estimates only with strong assumptions about the homogeneity of treatment effects, and may be biased when treatment effects vary over time or by adoption cohort
We study the dynamics of treatment effects using the interaction weighted (IW) estimator proposed in Sun and Abraham (2021).
task: In this paper, we study the adoption of a generative AI tool that provides conversational guidance for customer support agents.

The tool we study is built on a recent version of the Generative Pre-trained Transformer (GPT) family of large language models developed by OpenAI (OpenAI, 2023). It monitors customer chats and provides agents with real-time suggestions for how to respond.
It is designed to augment agents, who remain responsible for the conversation and are free to ignore its suggestions.

Once deployed, the AI system generates two main types of output: 1) real-time suggestions for how agents should respond to customers and 2) links to the data firm's internal documentation for relevant technical issues.
Chats are randomly assigned, and support sessions are relatively lengthy, averaging 40 minutes, with much of the conversation spent trying to diagnose the underlying technical problem.
The job requires a combination of detailed product knowledge, problem solving skills, and the ability to deal with frustrated customers.
population: We work with a company that provides AI-based customer service support software (hereafter, the "AI firm") to study the deployment of their tool at one of their client firms, (hereafter, the "data firm").
Our data firm is a Fortune 500 enterprise software company that specializes in business process software for small and medium-sized businesses in the United States.
It employs a variety of chat-based technical support agents, both directly and through third-party firms.
The majority of agents in our sample work from offices located in the Philippines, with a smaller group working in the United States and in other countries.
In total, we observe the conversation text and outcomes associated with 3 million chats by 5,179 agents.
Within this, we observe 1.2 million chats by 1,636 agents in the post-AI period.
Most agents in our sample, 89%, are located outside of the United States, primarily in the Philippines.
For each agent, we observe their assigned manager, tenure, geographic location, and firm information.
arms: We divide agents into three groups: agents who are never given access to the AI tool during our sample period ("never treated"), pre-AI observations for those who are eventually given access ("treated, pre"), and post-AI observations ("treated, post").
The AI assistant we study was gradually rolled out at the agent level after an initial seven-week randomized pilot featuring 50 agents.
The deployment was largely uniform across both the data firm's own customer service agents and its outsourced agents.
outcome_definitions: Our primary measure of productivity is resolutions per hour (RPH), the number of chats a worker is able to successfully resolve per hour.

An agent's RPH is determined by several factors: the average time it takes an agent to complete a conversation, the number of conversations they are able to handle per hour (accounting for multiple simultaneous conversations), and the share of conversations that are successfully resolved.
We measure these individually as, respectively, average handle time (AHT), chats per hour (CPH), and resolution rate (RR).
In addition, we also observe a measure of customer satisfaction through an agent's net promoter score (NPS), which is collected by the firm from post-call customer surveys.

Our firm measures productivity using three metrics that are standard in the customer service industry: "average handle time," the average time an agent takes to finish a chat; "resolution rate," the share of conversations that the agent successfully resolves; and "net promoter score," (customer satisfaction), which is calculated by randomly surveying customers after a chat and calculating the percentage of customers who would recommend an agent minus the percentage who would not.
We measure "adherence" starting at the chat level, using the share of AI recommendations that each agent follows.
Our AI firm codes agents as having adhered to a recommendation if they either click to copy the suggested AI text or if they self-input something very similar.
We attempt to capture the affective nature of both agent and customer text, using sentiment analysis (Mejova, 2009).
Sentiment is measured on a scale from −1 to 1, where −1 indicates negative sentiment and 1 indicates positive.
The outcome variable we focus on is the share of an agent's chats in which a customer requests to speak to a manager or supervisor, aggregated to the year-month level.
statistical_results: Access to the tool increases productivity, as measured by issues resolved per hour, by 14% on average, including a 34% improvement for novice and low-skilled workers but with minimal impact on experienced and highly skilled workers.
We find that access to AI assistance increases the productivity of agents by 14%, as measured by the number of customer issues they are able to resolve per hour.
In Column 1, we show that, controlling for time and location fixed effects, access to AI recommendations increases resolutions per hour by 0.47 chats, up 22.2% from an average of 2.12.
In Column 2, we include fixed effects for individual agents to account for potential differences between treated and untreated agents.

In Column 3, we include additional controls for the time-varying agent tenure. As we add controls, our effects fall slightly, so that, with agent and tenure fixed effects, we find that the deployment of AI increases RPH by 0.30 chats or 13.8%.
Column 1 documents a 3.8 minute decrease in the average duration of customer chats, a 9% decline from the baseline mean (shorter handle times are generally considered better).
Next, Column 2 indicates a 0.37 unit increase in the number of chats that an agent can handle per hour. Relative to a baseline mean of 2.6, this represents an increase of roughly 14%.
Column 3 of Table 3 indicates a small 1.3 percentage point increase in chat resolution rates, significant at the 10% level.
Finally, Column 4 finds no economically significant change in customer satisfaction, as measured by net promoter scores: the coefficient is -0.13 percentage points and the mean is 79.6%.

In Panel A, we show that the productivity impact of AI assistance is most pronounced for workers in the lowest skill quintile (leftmost side), who see a .29 log point or 34% increase in resolutions per hour.
In contrast, AI assistance does not lead to any productivity increase for the most skilled workers (rightmost side).
Agents with less than 1 month of tenure improve their resolutions per hour by .38 log points or 46% improvement (relative to agents of the same tenure who do not have access to AI assistance).
In contrast, we see no effect for agents with more than a year of tenure.

Across many of the outcomes in Figure 8, agents with two months of tenure and access to AI assistance perform as well as or better than agents with more than six months of tenure who do not have access.
The average adherence rate is 38%, with an interquartile range of 23% to 50%: agents frequently ignore recommendations.

We find a steady and monotonic increase in returns by agent adherence: among agents in the lowest quintile, we still see a 10% gain in productivity, but for agents in the highest quintile, the estimated impact is over twice as high, close to 25%.
Those in the bottom tercile initially follow recommendations less than 20% of the time but, by month five, their compliance rates have increased by over 50%, to just over half of the time.
More senior workers are initially less likely to follow AI recommendations: 30% for those with more than a year of tenure compared to 37% for those with less than three months of tenure.
Over time, however, all workers increase their adherence, with more senior workers doing so faster so that the groups converge five months after deployment.
For context, the sentences "Can you help me with logging in?" and "Why is my login not working?" have a cosine similarity of 0.68 in our model.
We find that textual similarity to the pre-AI window is stable in the weeks leading up to the AI roll-out and drops immediately following AI deployment.

We find that lower-skill agents (those in the bottom quintile of the pre-AI skill distribution) experience greater textual change after AI adoption, relative to top performers (those in the top quintile).
Among agents without AI access, we define high- and low-skill agents as those who are in the top or bottom quintile of our skill index for that month.
Focusing on the blue dots, we see that the average textual similarity between high- and low-productivity workers is 0.55 among workers who do not have access to AI assistance.

Turning to the red diamonds, we see that, post-AI adoption, high- and low-skilled workers begin to use language that is more similar. The magnitude of this change—moving from 0.55 similarity to 0.61 similarity—may appear small, but given that the average within-person similarity for high-skill workers is around 0.67, this result suggests that AI assistance is associated with a substantial narrowing of language gaps.
According to Column 1 of Table 4, access to AI improves the mean customer sentiments (averaged over an agent-month) by 0.18 points, equivalent to half of a standard deviation.
Column 2 of Table 4 indicates that agent sentiments increase by only 0.02 points or about 1% of a standard deviation.
Relative to a baseline rate of approximately 6 percentage points, these coefficients suggest that AI assistance generates an almost 25% decline in customer requests to speak to a manager.
The magnitude of this coefficient, around 10 percentage points, translates into a 40% decrease relative to a baseline attrition rate in this group of 25%.
For this analysis, we compare attrition rates among treated agents to those of untreated agents with the same tenure.
Panel A shows that access to AI assistance is associated with the strongest reductions in attrition among newer agents, those with less than 6 months of experience.
Never-treated agents resolve an average of 1.7 chats per hour, whereas post-treatment agents resolve 2.5 chats per hour.
Treated agents have higher resolutions per hour prior to AI model deployment (2.0 chats) relative to never treated agents (1.7).
Pre-treatment and never-treated agents have similar distributions of average handle times, centered at 40 minutes, but post-treatment agents have a lower average handle time of 35 minutes.
For this analysis, we examine how workers perform during periods in which they are not able to access AI-recommendations due to technical issues at the AI firm.
We see an immediate decline in the duration of individual chats by approximately 10% to 15%.

Panel B shows that the benefit of exposure to AI assistance increases with time during outage periods. That is, if an outage occurs one month after AI adoption, workers do not handle the chat much more quickly than their pre-adoption baseline. Yet, if an outage occurs after three months of exposure to AI recommendations, workers handle the chat faster—even though they are not receiving direct AI assistance.
Panel A shows that workers with high initial AI adherence see large and fast declines in chat processing times (relative to their pre-adoption baseline), even during outages.
Panel B, in contrast, shows no such impact for workers who tend to deviate from AI recommendations: they see no improvement in chat times during outage periods, even after many months of AI access.
ambiguity_or_caveats: Our overall findings show that access to generative AI can increase the productivity and retention of individual workers. We emphasize, however, that our paper is not designed to shed light on the aggregate employment or wage effects of generative AI tools.

Firms may respond to increasing productivity among novice workers by hiring more of them, de-skilling positions, or seeking to develop more powerful AI systems that can replace lower-skill workers entirely.
Unfortunately, our data do not allow us to observe changes in wages, overall labor demand, or the skill composition of workers hired for the job.

Our results also highlight the longer-term incentive challenges that AI systems bring. Top workers are generally not paid for their contributions to the training data that AI systems use to capture and disseminate their skills.
Yet, without these contributions, AI systems may be less effective in learning to resolve new problems.
Our work therefore raises questions about how workers should be compensated for the data they provide to AI systems.

Because our data firm outsources most of its customer service functions, it does not have direct control over this information, which is kept by subcontracted firms. As a result, we observe resolution rates and net promoter scores for a subset of agents in our data.
This, in turn, means that we only observe our omnibus productivity measure—resolutions per hour—for this smaller subset.

We note, however, that this relationship could also be driven by other factors: selection (agents who choose to adhere are more productive for other reasons); or selection on gains (agents who follow recommendations are those with the greatest returns).
An alternative hypothesis, however, is that agents who dislike working with AI assistance exit the firm at higher rates.
Because AI outages are rare and not necessarily random, this may reflect differences in the types of chats that are seen during outage periods than during non-outage periods.

We note that these results should be taken with more caution relative to our main results because we are unable to include agent fixed effects to control for unobservable differences between agents with and without access to AI assistance.

This is because attrition can only occur once for any given individual. Our results may overstate the impact of AI access on attrition if, for example, access to the AI tool is more likely to be given to agents whom the firm believes are more likely to stay.
Most importantly, our results do not capture the potential longer-term impacts of generative AI on skill demand, job design, wages, or customer demand.
It is unclear, for example, whether improvements in customer service productivity will lead to more or less demand for customer service workers.
If the demand for customer support is inelastic, then generative AI tools may reduce demand and wages in this sector in the long run.

Alternatively, better product support could lead customers to seek out representatives for a wider range of questions; this, in turn, could increase demand for workers or give them new responsibilities, such as collecting customer feedback for the product development team (Berg et al., 2018; Korinek, 2022).

Finally, as a potential general-purpose technology, generative AI can and will be deployed in a variety of ways, and the effects we find may not generalize across all firms and production processes (Eloundou et al., 2023).

For example, our setting has a relatively stable product and a set of technical support questions. In areas where the product or environment is changing rapidly, the relative value of AI recommendations may be different: they may be better able to synthesize changing best practices, or they may actually impede learning by promoting outdated practices observed in historical training data.

01a_quotes.notes: This is an empirical study of generative AI deployment in a real-world workplace setting. The paper uses a staggered difference-in-differences design to estimate causal effects of AI tool access on customer support agent productivity and worker outcomes. The study includes multiple outcome measures (productivity, customer sentiment, attrition) and explores heterogeneous treatment effects by worker skill and experience. The paper also investigates mechanisms through adherence analysis, learning effects during system outages, and textual analysis of agent communications.

01_extraction.title: Generative AI at Work
01_extraction.study_design_description: The study examines the staggered introduction of a generative AI-based conversational assistant using data from 5,179 customer support agents at a Fortune 500 software firm. The AI assistant was gradually rolled out at the agent level after an initial seven-week randomized pilot featuring 50 agents. The authors isolate the causal impact of access to AI recommendations using a standard difference-in-differences regression and employ the interaction weighted (IW) estimator to study the dynamics of treatment effects.
01_extraction.primary_task_description: The study focuses on the adoption of a generative AI tool built on a recent version of the Generative Pre-trained Transformer (GPT) family of large language models developed by OpenAI. The tool monitors customer chats and provides agents with real-time suggestions for how to respond, generating two main types of output: 1) real-time suggestions for how agents should respond to customers and 2) links to the data firm's internal documentation for relevant technical issues. The tool is designed to augment agents, who remain responsible for the conversation and are free to ignore its suggestions. Support sessions are relatively lengthy, averaging 40 minutes, with much of the conversation spent trying to diagnose the underlying technical problem.
01_extraction.population_description: The study observes conversation text and outcomes associated with 3 million chats by 5,179 customer support agents working for a Fortune 500 enterprise software company that specializes in business process software for small and medium-sized businesses in the United States. Within this, 1.2 million chats are observed by 1,636 agents in the post-AI period. The majority of agents in the sample work from offices located in the Philippines (89% located outside of the United States), with a smaller group working in the United States and in other countries. Agents work both directly for the firm and through third-party firms.

arms.description: Agents who are never given access to the AI tool during the sample period
Pre-AI observations for agents who are eventually given access to the AI tool
Post-AI observations for agents who are given access to the AI tool

outcomes.outcome_description: The number of chats a worker is able to successfully resolve per hour (RPH). An agent's RPH is determined by several factors: the average time it takes an agent to complete a conversation, the number of conversations they are able to handle per hour (accounting for multiple simultaneous conversations), and the share of conversations that are successfully resolved.
The average time an agent takes to finish a chat (AHT)
The number of conversations an agent is able to handle per hour (CPH), accounting for multiple simultaneous conversations
The share of conversations that the agent successfully resolves (RR)

Customer satisfaction measured by net promoter score (NPS), calculated by randomly surveying customers after a chat and calculating the percentage of customers who would recommend an agent minus the percentage who would not

The share of AI recommendations that each agent follows. Agents are coded as having adhered to a recommendation if they either click to copy the suggested AI text or if they self-input something very similar.

The affective nature of customer text, measured using sentiment analysis on a scale from -1 to 1, where -1 indicates negative sentiment and 1 indicates positive sentiment, averaged over an agent-month
The affective nature of agent text, measured using sentiment analysis on a scale from -1 to 1, where -1 indicates negative sentiment and 1 indicates positive sentiment
The share of an agent's chats in which a customer requests to speak to a manager or supervisor, aggregated to the year-month level
Whether an agent leaves the firm
Cosine similarity of agent language to pre-AI window, measuring the degree to which agent text resembles their pre-AI communication patterns

01_extraction.notes: This is a staggered difference-in-differences study of generative AI deployment in a real-world customer support setting. The paper uses multiple estimation approaches including standard DiD regression and the interaction weighted (IW) estimator to account for heterogeneous treatment effects over time and by adoption cohort. The study includes 5,179 agents total, with 1,636 agents observed in the post-AI period. The paper reports heterogeneous treatment effects by worker skill quintile and tenure, and explores mechanisms through adherence analysis, learning effects during system outages, and textual analysis. The authors note several caveats: (1) the data do not allow observation of changes in wages, overall labor demand, or skill composition of workers hired; (2) resolution rates and NPS are observed only for a subset of agents due to outsourcing; (3) the attrition analysis cannot include agent fixed effects because attrition occurs only once per individual, potentially overstating effects if AI access is more likely given to agents the firm believes are more likely to stay; (4) results may not generalize across all firms and production processes; and (5) the setting has a relatively stable product and technical support questions, so effects may differ in rapidly changing environments.

02_method.rationale: The study uses a 'staggered introduction' or 'staggered rollout' of the AI assistant and employs 'difference-in-differences regression' and 'interaction weighted (IW) estimator' to isolate causal effects. This is a quasi-experimental identification strategy analyzing the gradual deployment across 5,179 agents, distinct from the small initial 50-agent randomized pilot.

per_outcome.operationalization: count of successfully resolved customer chats per agent per hour, accounting for simultaneous conversations
mean duration in minutes from chat start to completion per agent
count of customer conversations handled per agent per hour, including simultaneous chats
percentage of agent conversations that result in successful resolution of the customer issue
customer satisfaction metric: percentage who would recommend the agent minus percentage who would not, from post-chat surveys
percentage of AI recommendations followed by agent, coded as copy-click or self-input of similar text
sentiment analysis score of customer text on -1 to 1 scale, averaged over agent-month
sentiment analysis score of agent text on -1 to 1 scale
percentage of agent chats where customer requests to speak to manager, aggregated to year-month level
percentage of agents who leave the firm
cosine similarity of agent text to pre-AI baseline communication patterns, scale 0-1

04_onet.rationale: The participants are customer support agents handling technical support chats for enterprise software. The task description explicitly states they are 'customer support agents' conducting 'support sessions' where they diagnose technical problems and respond to customers. This matches the occupation of Customer Service Representatives, who interact with customers to provide information and resolve complaints. While the work involves some technical troubleshooting, the core activity is customer service communication rather than technical repair or software development.

alternates.onet_label: Communicating with Supervisors, Peers, or Subordinates

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: This is a natural experiment with staggered rollout. The human-only baseline is 'never_treated' (agents who never received AI access during the study period). The AI arm is 'treated_post' (agents after receiving AI tool access). The 'treated_pre' arm represents the same agents before treatment and is used for difference-in-differences estimation, but the primary comparison for the overall effect is treated_post vs never_treated.
llm_extracted.notes: This is a natural experiment with staggered AI tool rollout. The paper reports multiple specifications with different fixed effects. The overall effect (14% increase, from 1.7 to 2.5 chats/hour) is reported for the main comparison. More conservative estimates with agent and tenure fixed effects show 13.8% increase (0.30 chats). The paper also reports heterogeneous effects by skill quintile (34% for lowest, 0% for highest) and tenure (46% for <1 month, 0% for >1 year). I extracted the overall/average effect as the primary statistic. Sample size n is only reported for treated_post arm (1636); n for never_treated is not explicitly stated.
source_quotes: Access to the tool increases productivity, as measured by issues resolved per hour, by 14% on average, including a 34% improvement for novice and low-skilled workers but with minimal impact on experienced and highly skilled workers.
Treated agents have higher resolutions per hour prior to AI model deployment (2.0 chats) relative to never treated agents (1.7).
In Column 1, we show that, controlling for time and location fixed effects, access to AI recommendations increases resolutions per hour by 0.47 chats, up 22.2% from an average of 2.12.
As we add controls, our effects fall slightly, so that, with agent and tenure fixed effects, we find that the deployment of AI increases RPH by 0.30 chats or 13.8%.

The paper also reports heterogeneous effects by skill quintile (34% for lowest, 0% for highest) and tenure (46% for <1 month, 0% for >1 year). I extracted the overall/average effect as the primary statistic. Sample size n is only reported for treated_post arm (1636); n for never_treated is not explicitly stated. | SDs/SEs missing; variance not computable

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: treated_post is the only arm with AI access. Comparing against never_treated (pure control) rather than treated_pre to avoid confounding from time trends and selection into treatment timing.
llm_extracted.notes: Resolution rate means extracted from Table 1 summary statistics. Never Treated arm shows resolution rate of .78, Treated Post shows .84. The prior pass missed that Table 1 provides the raw resolution rate means for each group. Sample sizes also from Table 1: Never Treated n=3,523 agents, Treated Post n=1,636 agents. No SDs are reported for resolution rate in the paper. The regression result in Table 3 Column 3 (0.0128* increase, p<.10) is consistent with the raw difference of 0.06 (0.84 - 0.78). Without SDs, we can only compute a rough effect size estimate, hence medium confidence. The p-value reported in Table 3 is an inequality (p < .10), not an exact value.
